summary: Manage DNS records (modeled for the moment DNS is brought in-repo): add a simple A/AAAA/CNAME/TXT record, add an alias record pointing at an ALB or CloudFront distribution, re-point an existing record's value, tune a record's TTL (self-service), shift traffic by adjusting a weighted record's weight, and delete a confirmed-unused record (irreversible). Terraform applies are instant; the real-world effect is TTL-bound propagation, called out on each change or delete.

operations.title: Add a DNS record (A/AAAA/CNAME/TXT)
operations.summary: Add a new DNS record. Reversible, but once it propagates, a wrong value quietly sends traffic to the wrong place.
operations.description: Creates a new simple (non-alias) record in the selected hosted zone. Additive and reversible, but the name becomes resolvable once the change propagates (TTL-bound), so a wrong value silently points clients somewhere unintended.

params.name: zone
params.label: Hosted zone
params.help: The hosted zone the new record is created in.

params.name: record_name
params.label: Record name (FQDN)

bounds.semantic: must be within the selected zone's domain

params.help: Fully-qualified name, e.g. api.internal.example.com. Must sit inside the chosen zone.

params.name: record_type
params.label: Record type
params.help: Simple record types only. Alias records use the dedicated Add-alias operation.

params.name: records
params.label: Record value(s)

bounds.semantic: each value must be valid for the chosen type: IPv4 for A, IPv6 for AAAA, a single FQDN for CNAME, a quoted string for TXT

params.help: One value per rdata entry. CNAME must be exactly one FQDN.

params.name: ttl
params.label: TTL (seconds)
params.help: How long resolvers cache this record. Lower = faster changes, more query volume.

operations.title: Add an alias record (to ALB/CloudFront)
operations.summary: Point a name at an AWS load balancer or CloudFront distribution. A wrong target misdirects traffic once it propagates.
operations.description: Creates an alias A or AAAA record that resolves to an AWS target such as an ALB or CloudFront distribution, with no TTL of its own. Additive and reversible; a wrong alias target DNS name or hosted-zone-id makes the name resolve incorrectly once propagated.

params.name: zone
params.label: Hosted zone
params.help: The hosted zone the alias record is created in.

params.name: record_name
params.label: Record name (FQDN)

bounds.semantic: must be within the selected zone's domain

params.help: Fully-qualified name, e.g. portal.example.com.

params.name: record_type
params.label: Record type
params.help: Alias records are A (IPv4) or AAAA (IPv6) only.

params.name: alias_target_dns_name
params.label: Alias target DNS name

bounds.semantic: the DNS name of an ALB, NLB, CloudFront distribution, or another Route53 record in this account

params.help: e.g. web-prod-alb-1234567890.ap-southeast-5.elb.amazonaws.com.

params.name: alias_target_zone_id
params.label: Alias target hosted zone ID
params.help: The canonical hosted zone ID of the target (ALB regional zone id, or Z2FDTNDATAQYW2 for CloudFront).

params.name: evaluate_target_health
params.label: Evaluate target health
params.help: Whether Route53 factors the target's health into responses.

operations.title: Change a record's value(s)
operations.summary: Change where an existing DNS record points. Once it propagates, all traffic to that name follows the new target until the cache expires.
operations.description: Re-points an existing record to new rdata value(s). Updates in place and is reversible, but every client that resolves the name after propagation follows the new target, so a mistake re-routes live traffic until the TTL expires.

params.name: record
params.label: Record
params.help: The record whose value(s) will change.

params.name: new_records
params.label: New value(s)

bounds.semantic: each value must be valid for the record's existing type; CNAME must be a single FQDN

params.help: Replaces the full set of rdata values for this record.

operations.title: Change a record's TTL
operations.summary: Change how long resolvers cache this record. Lowering it first makes a later value change take effect faster.
operations.description: Adjusts how long resolvers cache a record. Low-risk and reversible; a common pre-cutover step is lowering the TTL ahead of a planned value change so the switch propagates faster.

params.name: record
params.label: Record
params.help: The record whose TTL will change. Alias records have no TTL and are not eligible.

params.name: new_ttl
params.label: New TTL (seconds)
params.help: Cache lifetime in seconds. 60-300 is typical for records that change; 3600+ for stable records.

operations.title: Adjust a weighted record's weight
operations.summary: Shift the share of traffic this record receives in a weighted group, e.g. for a canary release or gradual cutover.
operations.description: Changes the weight on one record of a weighted routing set to shift the share of traffic it receives (e.g. canary or blue/green cutover). Reversible, but this moves live traffic proportionally once propagated, so it is reviewed before apply.

params.name: record
params.label: Weighted record
params.help: Must be a record that already has a weighted_routing_policy and a set_identifier.

params.name: new_weight
params.label: New weight
params.help: Relative weight vs the other records in the set. 0 drains this endpoint; traffic share = this weight / sum of all weights in the set.

operations.title: Delete a DNS record
operations.summary: Permanently delete this DNS record. Once propagated, the name stops resolving and breaks anything still using it. Confirm it's unused first.
operations.description: Permanently removes a record from its hosted zone. Irreversible in place - once propagated the name stops resolving (NXDOMAIN), so anything still pointed at it breaks until the record is recreated. Requires typed confirmation that the name is unused.

params.name: record
params.label: Record
params.help: The record to permanently delete.

params.name: confirm_unused
params.label: Confirm unused
params.help: Typed confirmation that no application, alias, health check, or downstream config still relies on this name resolving.

operations.title: Rename a Route 53 hosted zone (name)
operations.summary: Rename this hosted zone domain. Breaks delegation for every record inside until repointed — risk of a full outage. Engineer-reviewed.
operations.description: Changes the `name` argument of an existing hosted zone. The provider doc marks this in-place (not ForceNew), but it changes the domain the zone answers for and breaks NS delegation from the parent domain/registrar for every record inside it until delegation is repointed — broad blast radius, potential full outage. Engineer-only; a human reviews the real plan (which may show a replacement) before apply.

params.name: zone
params.label: Hosted zone
params.help: The hosted zone to rename.

params.name: new_name
params.label: New zone name (FQDN)

bounds.semantic: a valid lowercase DNS domain name

params.help: New domain name for the zone, e.g. example.com.

operations.downtime: resolution disruption for the zone's names until the parent NS delegation is repointed

operations.title: Change a hosted zone's reusable delegation set
operations.summary: Applying REPLACES this hosted zone with new name servers: every record is recreated and resolution breaks until delegation is repointed.
operations.description: Set `delegation_set_id`. CAUTION: ForceNew on aws_route53_zone — Terraform REPLACES the hosted zone: a new zone with new name servers, and every record must be recreated. DNS resolution is disrupted until the parent NS delegation is repointed. Engineer-authored change.

params.name: zone
params.label: Hosted zone
params.help: The public hosted zone whose delegation set will change.

params.name: delegation_set_id
params.label: Reusable delegation set
params.help: The reusable delegation set whose NS records will back the zone. Chosen from existing delegation sets in inventory.

operations.downtime: DNS resolution disruption — the hosted zone is replaced with new name servers; records are recreated and the parent NS delegation must be repointed

operations.title: Set forced destroy for a hosted zone
operations.summary: Allow a future zone deletion to also wipe out every record in it, even ones not managed here. No effect until that deletion happens.
operations.description: Sets the `force_destroy` flag on an existing hosted zone. When true, a later zone destroy will delete all records in the zone (possibly including records managed outside Terraform). The flag has no runtime effect on live resolution until a destroy actually occurs, but enabling it removes a deletion guardrail against irreversible data loss. Engineer-only.

params.name: zone
params.label: Hosted zone
params.help: The hosted zone to set force_destroy on.

params.name: force_destroy
params.label: Force destroy
params.help: true allows a subsequent zone destroy to remove all records in the zone; false keeps the guardrail.

operations.title: Associate an additional VPC with a private hosted zone (vpc)
operations.summary: Let another VPC resolve this private zone. The wrong VPC wrongly extends internal DNS to it. Engineer-reviewed.
operations.description: Adds a `vpc` block to an existing private hosted zone, associating another VPC so its resources resolve the zone's records. Additive, reversible (the association can later be removed), and non-disruptive to existing associations, but selecting the wrong VPC wrongly extends internal DNS to an unintended VPC — broad blast radius. Groups the tightly-coupled `vpc` block fields (`vpc_id` required, `vpc_region` optional). Private zones only — `vpc` conflicts with `delegation_set_id`. Engineer-only.

params.name: zone
params.label: Hosted zone
params.help: The private hosted zone to associate an additional VPC with.

params.name: vpc_id
params.label: VPC to associate
params.help: The VPC whose resources should resolve this private zone.

params.name: vpc_region
params.label: VPC region
params.help: AWS region code of the VPC (e.g. ap-southeast-5). Defaults to the provider region if omitted.

operations.title: Change a record's type
operations.summary: Change what type of DNS record this is, e.g. CNAME to A. Clients get a different answer; old cached answers may linger. Engineer-reviewed.
operations.description: Changes the `type` argument of an existing DNS record (e.g. CNAME to A). Bounded to the record types the provider documents as valid. Identity-changing: clients get a different answer for the same name and resolvers can hold stale or incompatible cached data during the transition, so every application is engineer-reviewed.

params.name: record
params.label: Record
params.help: The record whose type will change.

params.name: new_type
params.label: New record type
params.help: One of the DNS record types the aws_route53_record provider doc lists as valid.

operations.title: Set a failover record's PRIMARY/SECONDARY role
operations.summary: Set whether this record is the primary or backup target in a failover pair. A mismatched pair can make the name stop resolving.
operations.description: Sets `failover_routing_policy.type` on a record that already participates in a failover pair to PRIMARY or SECONDARY. Flipping the role changes which target serves all traffic for the name, and a mismatched pair (two PRIMARYs or two SECONDARYs) can black-hole the record, so every application is engineer-reviewed.

params.name: record
params.label: Failover record
params.help: Must be a record that already has a failover_routing_policy and a set_identifier.

params.name: failover_type
params.label: Failover role
params.help: PRIMARY is served while its health check passes, otherwise SECONDARY is served.

operations.title: Allow or block overwriting a pre-existing record
operations.summary: Allow or block a new record from overwriting an existing untracked one of the same name. Allowing it risks clobbering a live record.
operations.description: Sets the `allow_overwrite` flag, which controls whether a Terraform create may overwrite a pre-existing record of the same name and type that is not yet in state. Enabling it removes the guardrail against clobbering a live, possibly unrelated record; the provider defaults it to false and notes it is not recommended for most environments, so every application is engineer-reviewed.

params.name: record
params.label: Record
params.help: The record whose allow_overwrite flag will change.

params.name: allow_overwrite
params.label: Allow overwrite
params.help: true lets a Terraform create overwrite an existing record of the same name/type; false (default) blocks it.

operations.title: Toggle target health evaluation on an alias record
operations.summary: Turn health checks on or off for this alias record. When on, it stops pointing at its target while that target is unhealthy.
operations.description: Sets `alias.evaluate_target_health` on an existing alias record — whether Route 53 checks the alias target's health before answering with it. In-place, reversible behavior toggle; enabling it means the name stops resolving to the target while the target is unhealthy.

params.name: record
params.label: Alias record
params.help: Must be a record that already has an alias block.

params.name: evaluate_target_health
params.label: Evaluate target health
params.help: true makes Route 53 factor the alias target's health into its answer; false always answers with the target.

operations.title: Toggle multivalue answer routing
operations.summary: Turn multivalue-answer routing on or off, so resolvers can get several healthy answers back for this name at once.
operations.description: Sets `multivalue_answer_routing_policy` (true or false) on an existing record. When true, Route 53 returns a multivalue answer for the record; the provider documents that it conflicts with any other routing policy. In-place, reversible routing-behavior toggle.

params.name: record
params.label: Record
params.help: Must be a record with a set_identifier and no other routing policy attached.

params.name: multivalue_answer
params.label: Multivalue answer
params.help: true enables multivalue-answer routing for this record; false disables it.

operations.title: Set a geolocation record's continent
operations.summary: Set which continent this geolocation record answers for.
operations.description: Sets `geolocation_routing_policy.continent` on a record that already uses geolocation routing, changing which continent-level audience it answers for. Bounded to the seven continent codes Route 53 supports (per the GetGeoLocation API reference the provider doc cites).

params.name: record
params.label: Geolocation record
params.help: Must be a record that already has a geolocation_routing_policy and a set_identifier.

params.name: continent
params.label: Continent code
params.help: Two-letter Route 53 continent code: AF Africa, AN Antarctica, AS Asia, EU Europe, OC Oceania, NA North America, SA South America.

operations.title: Set a geolocation record's country
operations.summary: Set which country this geolocation record answers for, or make it the default for unmatched locations.
operations.description: Sets `geolocation_routing_policy.country` on a record that already uses geolocation routing. The provider doc constrains the value to a two-character ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code, or `*` for the default resource record set.

params.name: record
params.label: Geolocation record
params.help: Must be a record that already has a geolocation_routing_policy and a set_identifier.

params.name: country
params.label: Country code

bounds.semantic: two-character ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code (uppercase), or * for the default resource record set

params.help: e.g. ID, SG, US, or * for the default record.

operations.title: Set a geoproximity record's AWS region
operations.summary: Set the AWS region where this geoproximity record's target lives, used to calculate proximity routing.
operations.description: Sets `geoproximity_routing_policy.aws_region` on a record that already uses geoproximity routing — the AWS region where the target resource is present, used to compute proximity. Bounded to AWS region-code format.

params.name: record
params.label: Geoproximity record
params.help: Must be a record that already has a geoproximity_routing_policy and a set_identifier.

params.name: aws_region
params.label: AWS region

bounds.semantic: an AWS region code, e.g. ap-southeast-5

params.help: AWS region where the target resource is present, e.g. ap-southeast-5.

operations.title: Adjust a geoproximity record's bias
operations.summary: Expand or shrink the geographic area routed to this resource by adjusting its proximity bias.
operations.description: Sets `geoproximity_routing_policy.bias` on a record that already uses geoproximity routing, expanding or shrinking the geographic area routed to this resource. Bounded to the provider-documented range of -90 to 90.

params.name: record
params.label: Geoproximity record
params.help: Must be a record that already has a geoproximity_routing_policy and a set_identifier.

params.name: bias
params.label: Bias
params.help: Positive values route more traffic to this resource, negative values route less. Documented range -90 to 90.

operations.title: Set a latency record's AWS region
operations.summary: Set the AWS region Route 53 measures latency from for this record, used for latency-based routing.
operations.description: Sets `latency_routing_policy.region` on a record that already uses latency-based routing — the AWS region from which Route 53 measures latency for this record. Bounded to AWS region-code format.

params.name: record
params.label: Latency record
params.help: Must be a record that already has a latency_routing_policy and a set_identifier.

params.name: region
params.label: AWS region

bounds.semantic: an AWS region code, e.g. ap-southeast-5

params.help: AWS region from which latency is measured for this record, e.g. ap-southeast-5.

operations.title: Provision a new hosted zone
operations.summary: Request a brand-new public or private hosted zone; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it applies.
operations.description: Stand up a new aws_route53_zone as a complete specification: its domain name and, optionally, a VPC that makes it private. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review.

params.name: zone_name
params.label: Domain name
params.help: Fully-qualified domain the zone is authoritative for, e.g. internal.example.com.

params.name: comment
params.label: Comment
params.help: Optional free-text note shown in the console.

params.name: private_vpc
params.label: VPC (for a private zone)
params.help: Leave blank for a PUBLIC hosted zone. Pick a VPC to make this a PRIVATE hosted zone, resolvable only inside it.

params.name: force_destroy
params.label: Allow delete with records present
params.help: Always off for a new zone — deleting a zone that still holds records must be an explicit engineer follow-up, written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Hosted zone name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this zone is for
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this zone.

decisions: Leaving the VPC picker blank creates a PUBLIC hosted zone, resolvable from the internet once delegated; picking a VPC creates a PRIVATE zone resolvable only inside it — confirm which is intended before submitting
Associating additional VPCs afterward, and a custom/reusable delegation set, are engineer follow-ups (the existing associate-a-VPC action)
Records are added as a separate follow-up step (the existing add-a-record action) — a new zone starts with only its default NS/SOA records

operations.title: Provision a new DNS record (engineer-reviewed draft)
operations.summary: Author a new simple DNS record as a complete draft; an engineer reviews the Terraform before it applies.
operations.description: Stand up a new aws_route53_record as a complete specification: zone, name, type, value(s) and TTL. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review. For a routine addition to an established zone, prefer the existing self-service add-a-record action; use this form when a full engineer-reviewed draft is wanted instead.

params.name: record_key
params.label: Record identifier
params.help: Lower-case letters, numbers and dashes. Names the record in the generated draft — a DNS record has no separate name field of its own beyond its FQDN.

params.name: zone
params.label: Hosted zone
params.help: The hosted zone the new record is created in.

params.name: record_name
params.label: Record name (FQDN)

bounds.semantic: must be within the selected zone's domain

params.help: Fully-qualified name, e.g. api.internal.example.com. Must sit inside the chosen zone.

params.name: record_type
params.label: Record type
params.help: Simple record types only. Alias records use the existing add-alias-record action.

params.name: records
params.label: Record value(s)

bounds.semantic: each value must be valid for the chosen type: IPv4 for A, IPv6 for AAAA, a single FQDN for CNAME, a quoted string for TXT

params.help: One value per rdata entry. CNAME must be exactly one FQDN.

params.name: ttl
params.label: TTL (seconds)
params.help: How long resolvers cache this record. Lower = faster changes, more query volume.

decisions: A record change becomes live once it propagates (TTL-bound) and can silently redirect traffic to the wrong place — verify the value carefully before submitting

For a routine A/AAAA/CNAME/TXT addition to an established zone, the existing self-service add-a-record action stays the faster path; use this form when a full engineer-reviewed draft is wanted instead

Alias records and non-simple routing policies (weighted, latency, failover, geolocation, geoproximity) are engineer decisions — use the existing add-alias-record action or the dedicated set-* actions after creation
